title: Venus Just Gave Up a Secret: A Lava Tube Bigger Than Any on Earth
summary:
Venus is often called Earth's "sister planet"—it's similar in size and relatively close. But its surface is hidden beneath thick, toxic clouds. Now, scientists have peeled back one more layer of mystery by discovering a massive underground lava tube there.

This isn't just any cave. It's the first lava tube ever detected on Venus, and it's huge—potentially up to 1 kilometer wide. That's bigger than any lava tube found on Earth or Mars. The discovery came from a fresh analysis of radar images taken by NASA's Magellan probe in the early 1990s. Researchers spotted a collapsed "skylight"—a hole where the roof of the lava tube had caved in—near a broad volcano called Nyx Mons (named for the Greek goddess of night). The skylight is about 150 meters (490 feet) deep, and the tube itself extends at least 375 meters (1,230 feet) below the surface.

"It is remarkable that we are still extracting new insights from Magellan data," said Anna Gülcher, a planetary scientist at the University of Freiburg who wasn't involved in the study. She added that the finding points to "the lasting value of that mission, the progress we have made in data analysis and the renewed interest in the planet."

The discovery raises a fascinating puzzle: lava tubes on the Moon are large because of low gravity, but Venus has gravity similar to Earth's. So why is this tube so enormous? Scientists aren't sure yet.

This finding will influence two upcoming missions. NASA's VERITAS and the European Space Agency's EnVision, both scheduled to launch before 2031, will carry advanced radar instruments capable of imaging Venus's surface at much higher resolution than Magellan could. They may find even more lava tubes—and perhaps solve the mystery of how they formed.

Unfortunately, humans will never visit this particular cave. The surface pressure on Venus is 93 times that of Earth—enough to crush a submarine—and temperatures are hot enough to melt lead. But for scientists, the discovery is a thrilling reminder that even decades-old data can still reveal new wonders.
why_it_matters: Understanding how lava tubes form on different planets helps scientists refine models of volcanic activity and planetary geology, and could guide the search for safe habitats on the Moon and Mars.

keywords.explanation: Earth's 'sister planet' with a thick, toxic atmosphere and high surface pressure.
An underground tunnel formed by flowing lava; the first one detected on Venus.
A broad volcano on Venus named after the Greek goddess of night.
A NASA probe that mapped Venus with radar in the early 1990s.
A future NASA mission planned to image Venus at higher resolution.
A European Space Agency mission to study Venus with advanced radar.

questions.question: What is the potential width of the lava tube?
Which probe collected the radar data used in the discovery?
What feature did researchers spot that indicated a lava tube?
Why is the size of this lava tube puzzling?
options: Venus has Earth-like gravity, but the tube is as large as ones on the Moon
Venus has lower gravity than Earth
the tube is made of ice
the tube is on top of a volcano
questions.correct_answer: Venus has Earth-like gravity, but the tube is as large as ones on the Moon

questions.question: Which two upcoming missions will use advanced radar to study Venus?
What prevents humans from visiting this cave?
options: surface pressure 93 times Earth's and extreme heat
questions.correct_answer: surface pressure 93 times Earth's and extreme heat

background_read: NASA's Magellan mission (1990–1994) mapped 98% of Venus's surface using synthetic aperture radar. It revolutionized our understanding of Venusian geology but had limited resolution.
Lava tubes are common on Earth, the Moon, and Mars. On the Moon, large tubes can form because of low gravity. On Venus, with Earth-like gravity, such a large tube is unexpected.

The surface of Venus has a pressure of about 93 bars (equivalent to 900 meters deep in Earth's ocean) and temperatures around 460°C, hot enough to melt lead. No spacecraft has survived more than a few hours on the surface.

VERITAS (Venus Emissivity, Radio Science, InSAR, Topography, and Spectroscopy) and EnVision are planned to launch in the late 2020s/early 2030s. They will use advanced radar to create high-resolution maps of the surface.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Venus's hidden secret – giant lava tube discovered in old data
└─ hook: 'Earth's sister planet… another layer of mystery'
DISCOVERY: First lava tube ever detected on Venus
├─ HOW: Reanalysis of Magellan radar images showed a collapsed skylight
├─ SIZE: Skylight 150 m deep, tube at least 375 m deep, potentially 1 km wide
└─ QUOTE: Anna Gülcher on remarkable insights from old data
PUZZLE: Why is the tube so large?
├─ Lunar tubes are large due to low gravity, but Venus has Earth gravity
FUTURE MISSIONS: VERITAS and EnVision will provide high-res radar
CONSTRAINTS: Human visits impossible due to extreme pressure and heat
CONCLUSION: Even decades-old data can reveal new wonders

perspectives.description: This discovery proves that old datasets can still yield groundbreaking results when re-examined with new tools and questions, encouraging investment in data preservation.
The inference about the tube's size is based on a single skylight and indirect evidence; confirmation will require higher-resolution radar from future missions.
The mismatch between the tube's size and Venus's gravity highlights how little we know about Venusian volcanic processes. It may require revising models of lava tube formation.
VERITAS and EnVision will not only confirm this find but likely discover many more lava tubes, perhaps solving the puzzle and revealing new volcanic features.
